title: Rae Taylor and Trees (easy version)
description: You are given a permutation p of length n. Determine if there exists an undirected tree with n vertices labeled 1, 2, ..., n, satisfying the following condition: Let u and v (1 <= u < v <= n) be any two vertices connected by an edge. Then u appears before v in p.
input_spec: The first line contains a single integer t (1 <= t <= 10^4) -- the number of test cases. The first line of each test case contains a single integer n (2 <= n <= 2 * 10^5). The second line of each test case contains n integers, p_1, p_2, ..., p_n (1 <= p_i <= n). It is guaranteed that all p_i are distinct. It is guaranteed that the sum of n over all test cases does not exceed 2 * 10^5.
output_spec: For each test case, output on a single line "Yes" if there exists a tree satisfying the given condition, and "No" otherwise.

examples.output:
Yes
No
No
Yes
No
Yes
Yes
Yes
Yes

routes.name: Prefix-min / suffix-max connectivity check
routes.description: Compute prefix minimums and suffix maximums of the permutation. The tree exists if and only if for every split point, the minimum value seen so far is less than the maximum value remaining, guaranteeing an edge can always cross any partition.

observations.title: Restate the edge constraint as an ordering rule
observations.description: For every edge (u, v) with u < v, the smaller-labeled vertex u must appear earlier in p than v. This means every edge connects a value that appeared earlier to a larger value that appeared later. Thinking in terms of p's positions: if value a is at position i and value b > a is at position j, the edge (a, b) is only allowed when i < j.

hints.nudge: Rewrite the edge constraint purely in terms of which values can be adjacent in the tree. What does it say about the relationship between a value's magnitude and its position in p?
hints.question: If you pick two values a < b to be connected by an edge, what must be true about their positions in the permutation? Does this constraint apply to every edge, or just some?
hints.formal: An edge between vertices u and v (u < v) is allowed if and only if pos(u) < pos(v), where pos(x) is the index of x in p. Every edge of the tree must satisfy this. This defines a directed constraint: the smaller endpoint must appear earlier.

observations.title: A tree on n vertices must be connected across every partition
observations.description: In any tree, removing one edge splits the vertices into exactly two connected components. Equivalently, for the tree to exist, there must be at least one allowable edge crossing every possible bipartition of the vertex set into consecutive positions in p. The critical partitions are at positions i and i+1 in p: the values {p_1, ..., p_i} and {p_{i+1}, ..., p_n} must have at least one valid edge between them.

hints.nudge: If you split the permutation at some position, what must be true for the tree to connect values on both sides?
hints.question: Consider the values in positions 1..i and the values in positions (i+1)..n. For the tree to be connected, what kind of edge must exist between these two groups? What does the edge constraint require?
hints.formal: For any split at position i, the tree must have at least one edge (u, v) where u is among {p_1,...,p_i} and v is among {p_{i+1},...,p_n} with u < v. If no such pair exists for some i, the tree cannot be connected, so the answer is No.

observations.title: An allowed cross-partition edge exists iff prefix-min < suffix-max
observations.description: An edge between the left group {p_1,...,p_i} and right group {p_{i+1},...,p_n} requires some u in the left group and v in the right group with u < v. The best candidate is u = min(p_1,...,p_i) and v = max(p_{i+1},...,p_n). Such a valid edge exists if and only if pre_i < suf_{i+1}, where pre_i is the prefix minimum and suf_{i+1} is the suffix maximum.

hints.nudge: To check if any value on the left is smaller than any value on the right, what single representative from each side would you compare?
hints.question: If the smallest value on the left is still larger than the largest value on the right, can any valid edge cross the partition? What are the prefix-min and suffix-max telling you?
hints.formal: Define pre_i = min(p_1,...,p_i) and suf_i = max(p_i,...,p_n). A valid crossing edge at split i exists iff pre_i < suf_{i+1}. Reason: pre_i is in the left group and suf_{i+1} is in the right group; if pre_i < suf_{i+1}, the edge (pre_i, suf_{i+1}) is allowed. If pre_i > suf_{i+1}, then every left value exceeds every right value, so no valid edge exists.

observations.title: Check all split points in O(n) with prefix and suffix arrays
observations.description: Precompute the prefix minimum array and suffix maximum array in one pass each. Then sweep through all n-1 split points checking pre_i < suf_{i+1}. If every split passes, output Yes; if any fails, output No. The entire solution runs in O(n) time.

hints.nudge: You need to check a condition at every position. Can you precompute what you need in linear time?
hints.question: How do you compute prefix minimums incrementally? How about suffix maximums? Once you have both arrays, how many comparisons do you need?
hints.formal: Build pre[1..n] left to right: pre[1] = p[1], pre[i] = min(pre[i-1], p[i]). Build suf[1..n] right to left: suf[n] = p[n], suf[i] = max(suf[i+1], p[i]). For i = 1 to n-1: if pre[i] > suf[i+1], output No and stop. If all pass, output Yes. Time O(n), space O(n).

routes.name: Incremental connectivity via greedy parent assignment
routes.description: Process the permutation left to right. Each new value p_i needs a parent in the tree among the values already seen that are smaller than p_i. Track whether such a parent always exists by maintaining the minimum value seen so far and checking it against the values yet to appear.

observations.title: Build the tree by processing p left to right
observations.description: Process values in the order they appear in p. When p_i arrives, it can only attach to a previously-seen value that is smaller than p_i (since the smaller value appeared earlier, satisfying the edge constraint). The tree is constructible if every value (except possibly the global minimum, which must be the root of some subtree) can find such a parent.

hints.nudge: If you add vertices to the tree in the order given by p, what constraint determines which earlier vertex a new vertex can connect to?
hints.question: When value v arrives at position i, it needs an edge to some already-placed value u < v. What condition ensures such a u always exists among the previously placed values?
hints.formal: Process p_1, p_2, ..., p_n in order. Value p_i can attach as a child to any already-placed value u where u < p_i. If at some point no such u exists among the placed values, the tree cannot be built.

observations.title: The prefix minimum is the universal connector
observations.description: The minimum value seen so far (prefix minimum) has appeared earliest and is the smallest, making it an eligible parent for any subsequent larger value. If the prefix minimum at position i is smaller than a new value at position i+1, that new value can always attach to the prefix minimum.

hints.nudge: Among all values seen so far, which one can serve as parent for the widest range of future values?
hints.question: The smallest value seen so far appeared earlier than everything else and is smaller than most values. Can it be the parent of any new value that is larger?
hints.formal: Let pre_i = min(p_1,...,p_i). Any future value v > pre_i can use pre_i as its parent since pre_i appeared earlier and pre_i < v. The prefix minimum is the most universally connectable node.

observations.title: Failure occurs when all remaining values are smaller than everything seen
observations.description: A connectivity failure occurs at position i if every value in {p_{i+1},...,p_n} is smaller than every value in {p_1,...,p_i}. This means the suffix maximum suf_{i+1} is less than the prefix minimum pre_i, so no remaining value can attach to any seen value (or vice versa). This is the only way the construction fails.

hints.nudge: When exactly does the greedy construction break down? What relationship between past and future values causes a problem?
hints.question: If the largest value still to come is smaller than the smallest value already placed, can any future value connect to any past value?
hints.formal: The construction fails at split i iff max(p_{i+1},...,p_n) < min(p_1,...,p_i), i.e., suf_{i+1} < pre_i. When this holds, no edge can cross the boundary: every future value is smaller than every past value, so the tree would be disconnected.

observations.title: Reduce to the same O(n) prefix-min / suffix-max check
observations.description: Both the constructive argument and the partition argument lead to the identical condition: the tree exists if and only if pre_i < suf_{i+1} for all 1 <= i <= n-1. Compute both arrays in O(n) and verify.

hints.nudge: You have narrowed the failure condition to a comparison between prefix minimums and suffix maximums. How do you check this efficiently?
hints.question: Can you precompute both the prefix minimum and suffix maximum arrays and check all positions in one pass?
hints.formal: Compute pre[i] = min(p[1..i]) left-to-right and suf[i] = max(p[i..n]) right-to-left. Answer is Yes iff pre[i] < suf[i+1] for all i in [1, n-1]. Total time O(n), space O(n).
